title: Publications
text: A current list of peer-reviewed journal articles, edited books and chapters, and selected other scholarly outputs. For the most up-to-date list with citation counts, see Google Scholar. BibTeX for everything below is available in references.bib.

title: Peleg Kremer
text:
Associate Professor  ·  Villanova University


Spatial analysis for just and sustainable urban social-ecological systems. My lab uses GIS, remote sensing, and machine learning to understand how the structure and governance of cities shape who experiences environmental burdens and who receives environmental benefits, with a focus on urban heat, air quality, flooding, green stormwater infrastructure, and equitable access to green space in Philadelphia and beyond.


Research Publications CV







Active funded research




NSF SCC-IRG · Track 1


Integrating Community Dynamics, Environmental Data, and AI to Advance Green Stormwater Infrastructure Sustainability


Co-PI · 2024 to 2028




NSF DISES


Abating Mobility Equity Gaps Induced by Nuisance Flooding in Underserved Communities


Co-PI · 2024 to 2028




William Penn Foundation


Beyond Land Precarity: Building a Garden Database and Platform to Support Philadelphia Growers


PI · 2025 to 2027




What is new




2026 · Apr

Three Kremer-lab papers at the AAG annual meeting in San Francisco: PGDC’s approach to urban garden preservation, measuring community garden security in Philadelphia, and PM2.5 and chronic disease across 500 US cities.

2026 · Mar

Sensitivity of urban structure-temperature relationships to grid parameterization out in Ecological Informatics with Dennis Weaver and Justin Stewart.

2025 · Oct

WPF Webinar with Craig Borowiak: Roots at Risk: How Secure are Philadelphia Urban Gardens?

2025 · Jul

Began as Associate Editor of Sustainable Cities and Society.

2024 · Dec

Unequal access to social, environmental and health amenities in US urban parks published in Nature Cities (Winkler, Clark, Locke, Kremer et al.).


See all news

Research themes




STURLA


Urban structure and environmental conditions
How the three-dimensional structure of cities, the way buildings, vegetation, and pavement combine at the block scale, shapes the heat, air quality, and ecological processes residents experience.




Green infrastructure


Green stormwater infrastructure
Integrating spatial data with AI and community knowledge to plan, evaluate, and improve GSI in ways that are effective and equitable.




Urban flooding


Mobility and nuisance flooding
How chronic, low-grade flooding restructures everyday mobility and access to opportunity in underserved communities.




Community gardens


Philadelphia Garden Data Collaborative
A long-term partnership with civic organizations to protect community gardens through spatial data, monitoring, and policy translation.




Cross-cutting


Environmental justice and amenity access
Why access to nature in the city remains unequal, and what spatial frameworks could change that.

title: Teaching
text: I teach undergraduate and graduate courses in Geographic Information Systems, urban sustainability, and the built environment. My approach is learning-centered and project-based, with most courses producing applied spatial analyses that connect to ongoing research or to community partners.

title: Students
text: Working with students is one of the most rewarding parts of this job. Below are current advisees and alumni, with brief project descriptions. If you are a prospective student interested in spatial analysis of urban systems, please be in touch.

title: Students
text:

Sarah Scheller – Impacts of gentrification on garden land security.

2023

title: Environmental Justice and Equitable Access to Urban Amenities
text:
Program of work

2013 to present



Key vehicle

SESYNC “Parks for People” research pursuit



Lead collaborators

Lepczyk, Aronson, Pearsall, Locke, Clark, Winkler, Vargo, Lerman, Nilon, Hoover, Joo, Lee, La Rosa, Larson

title: Environmental Justice and Equitable Access to Urban Amenities
text:
The question
Urban green spaces, parks, and environmental amenities are unequally distributed within and across US cities, often along lines of race, income, and historical disinvestment. Why does access to nature in the city remain so unequal, and what spatial frameworks could change that?

title: Environmental Justice and Equitable Access to Urban Amenities
text:
What we do
This program combines conceptual synthesis with empirical work. Earlier papers articulated the apparent paradox between urban greening and social inclusion (Haase et al. 2017, Habitat International) and made the case for centering equity in urban ecosystem services research (Kremer et al. 2016, Ecology and Society). Through the SESYNC “Parks for People” research pursuit, the collaboration has been producing a sequence of empirical papers on park access:

National scale. Winkler, Clark, Locke, Kremer et al. (2024, Nature Cities) examines unequal access to social, environmental, and health amenities in US urban parks, showing that disparities are not only about whether parks exist but about what they contain.
Park quality measurement. Lee et al. (2024, Landscape and Urban Planning) reviews park-quality measurement instruments and proposes directions for the next generation of tools.
Human–nature interactions. Joo, Clark, Kremer, Aronson (2024, Urban Ecosystems) examines socio-environmental drivers of human–nature interactions in urban green spaces.
Local grounding. Scolio, Bohra, Kremer, Shakya (2024, Atmosphere) maps fine-scale particulate air pollution disparities in Philadelphia onto historical patterns of redlining and disinvestment.




Fine-scale particulate air pollution disparities in Philadelphia overlaid on historical patterns of redlining and disinvestment (Scolio, Bohra, Kremer, Shakya 2024, Atmosphere).

title: Environmental Justice and Equitable Access to Urban Amenities

title: Mobility Equity Gaps and Nuisance Flooding
text:
Funder

NSF DISES



Period

2024 to 2028



My role

Co-PI



Team

Ermagun & Xiong (Co-PIs), Smith, Kremer, Wadzuk (Supporting), Levy (Co-PI)

title: Mobility Equity Gaps and Nuisance Flooding
text:
The question
Nuisance flooding (chronic, low-grade flooding from drainage failure, sea level rise, and intense precipitation) is increasingly common in US cities, but its everyday consequences for the people who live with it are poorly understood. How does this kind of flooding restructure mobility, access to opportunity, and exposure to risk in underserved communities, and how can transportation and stormwater planning be reoriented to close those gaps?

title: Mobility Equity Gaps and Nuisance Flooding
text:
What this project does
A Dynamics of Integrated Socio-Environmental Systems (DISES) award combining transportation engineering, civil engineering, geography, and community partners. My lab contributes the geospatial analysis of flood-mobility coupling and the equity framing that connects environmental exposure to mobility disruption. Field sites include Philadelphia neighborhoods with documented nuisance-flooding histories.



Urban runoff modeling component of the integrated framework (Marks et al. 2022).

title: Mobility Equity Gaps and Nuisance Flooding

title: Green Stormwater Infrastructure: Community, Data, AI
text:
The question
Green stormwater infrastructure (GSI) is increasingly central to municipal stormwater strategy, but its performance varies widely with site conditions, maintenance, and the social context in which it is embedded. How can cities integrate spatial data with AI and community knowledge to plan, evaluate, and improve GSI in ways that are both effective and equitable?

title: Green Stormwater Infrastructure: Community, Data, AI
text:
What this project does
A team of civil engineers, computer engineers, geographers, and community partners working together on a community-integrated AI framework for GSI sustainability. My lab contributes the geospatial analysis and the integration of social and environmental data with the engineering and hydrologic models. The project builds on a prior NSF planning grant (2022–2023) and a CDS&E award on AI for sustainable and fair GSI (2022–2025).



GSI maintenance vulnerability across Philadelphia (Homet, Kremer, Smith, Strader 2022, Frontiers in Environmental Science).

title: Philadelphia Garden Data Collaborative
text:
The collaborative
The Philadelphia Garden Data Collaborative (PGDC) is a partnership between academics and Philadelphia civic organizations to:

Generate and maintain an authoritative spatial dataset of currently active community gardens in Philadelphia.
Keep that dataset current and accurate through field verification and remote sensing.
Use it to promote and protect public access to open and green spaces in Philadelphia.

In my role with PGDC, I work with students to verify active garden locations, maintain a GIS database, provide spatial analysis and maps as required by partners. Recent applications include maps and analysis of gardens by community district for hearings in the Philadelphia City Council on urban agriculture, and identification of active gardens located on tax-delinquent parcels at risk of loss through sheriff sales.

title: Philadelphia Garden Data Collaborative
text:
What the current grants support
“Beyond Land Precarity” (2025–2027) extends PGDC’s empirical and policy work into the construction of an active garden database and a public platform that growers, civic organizations, and city agencies can use.

title: Urban Structure and Environmental Conditions
text:
Program of work

2014 to present



NSF support

2018 to 2024



My role

PI on NSF GSS award; lead methodologist



Key collaborators

Dagmar Haase (Humboldt), Neele Larondelle, Zoe Hamstead, Timon McPhearson, Kabindra Shakya (Villanova Chemistry)

title: Urban Structure and Environmental Conditions
text:
The question
How does the three-dimensional structure of cities, the way buildings, vegetation, and pavement combine at the block scale, shape the heat, air quality, and ecological processes that residents actually experience? Most urban environmental analyses rely on flat, two-dimensional land cover and miss this vertical dimension.

title: Urban Structure and Environmental Conditions
text:
What we do
At the heart of this work is the Structure of Urban Landscapes (STURLA) classification, which captures common three-dimensional compositions of urban form and links them to environmental indicators. The framework was first developed in a Berlin–New York City comparison (Larondelle et al. 2014), refined for New York City (Hamstead et al. 2016), and extended to Philadelphia (Mitz et al. 2021). In parallel, with Dr. Kabindra Shakya in Villanova Chemistry, we built a mobile platform for fine-scale air pollution monitoring (Shakya et al. 2019).
Current work pushes STURLA in several directions:

From surface temperature to the air temperature people experience. Madeline Scolio led work linking remotely sensed surface temperature to air temperature, opening a path to use globally available remote sensing for urban heat exposure assessment (Scolio et al. 2024, Urban Climate).
Grid sensitivity. Dennis Weaver and Justin Stewart tested 399 grid configurations and found that grid size, not orientation, drives results, with the tightest urban structure-function relationships at roughly 200 to 500 meters (Kremer, Weaver, Stewart 2026, Ecological Informatics). The finding has implications well beyond STURLA.
Global scale. A current single-author paper applies STURLA via Google Earth Engine to 11,214 urban areas across 178 countries, testing whether case-study temperature patterns generalize worldwide and surfacing climate-dependent cooling effects of trees and water (Kremer, in review, Urban Climate).




Surface temperature to air temperature relationship in the Philadelphia metropolitan area (Scolio et al. 2024, Urban Climate).





Sensitivity of urban structure-temperature R² to grid size across 399 grid configurations (Kremer, Weaver, Stewart 2026, Ecological Informatics).

title: Research
text: We use GIS, remote sensing, and machine learning to study how cities shape exposure to environmental burdens (heat, flooding, air pollution) and access to environmental benefits (parks, cooling, green space). Most fieldwork is in Philadelphia, with comparative work across US and global cities.

title: About
text:
Dr. Peleg Kremer is an Associate Professor in the Department of Geography and the Environment at Villanova University, where she also serves as Program Director for Environmental Studies. Her lab focuses on spatial patterns in social-ecological systems and urban sustainability. Using interdisciplinary approaches in spatial analysis, GIS, and remote sensing, she investigates the relationships between urban environmental and ecological processes and the built environment, with active programs of work on urban heat, air quality, flooding, green stormwater infrastructure, and equitable access to green space in Philadelphia and other US cities.
She is currently Co-PI on four NSF awards and two William Penn Foundation grants, Associate Editor of Sustainable Cities and Society, and recipient of the 2023 Villanova Outstanding Faculty Mentor Award. She teaches undergraduate and graduate courses in Geographic Information Systems, urban sustainability, and the built environment, and has mentored more than two dozen graduate and undergraduate researchers since 2022.
She holds a PhD in Energy and Environmental Policy from the University of Delaware.

title: Contact
text:
For prospective students
I advise master’s students in the GEV graduate program and serve on dissertation committees across Villanova. Strong candidates typically have a background in geography, environmental science, planning, or a related field, and experience with GIS or programming (R or Python). Funded research assistantships are sometimes available through active grants; please email with a CV and a one-paragraph statement of interest.
